text: garden. The quiet and the birdsong. And I was really excited to map it. I already knew what typefaces I was going to use, how I was going to draw it. But it was more than that. I can’t explain it very well, but I felt like something was waiting for me there. And I really, really wanted to go there.’ ‘What sort of thing?’ ‘Peace, I suppose. I felt like peace was waiting for me there.’ ‘Well, that’s ... I mean peace is always good.’ Laughs. ‘It’s silly. I know it’s silly. So, as I said, we started making plans. Val said to wear old clothes and strong shoes. I suppose the garden had got a bit overgrown. We were going to take lunch with us and Val said to wrap up warm because it got cold. We set a date. We were going to meet at his house. But a few days before we were due to go he rang and cancelled.’ ‘Did he say why?’ ‘He said he’d found someone else.’ ‘Another cartographer?’ ‘He said it wasn’t a cartographer. It was someone who could get other kinds of information about the maze.’ ‘What information?’ ‘T honestly have no clue. I said something like: “I thought the whole idea was to make a map.” And he said that was one of the ideas, but not the only one.’ ‘What do you think that meant?’ ‘I don’t know. Honestly I was so pissed off.’ ‘I don’t blame you.’ ‘He’d just been wasting my time. And I’d been so looking forward to making my map.’ Silence. ‘And he definitely said that it was a maze?’ Pause. ‘Actually I think the word he used was “labyrinth”. But it’s the same thing, isn’t it?’ ‘And you said that you set a date to visit the maze. Would you be able to find out when that was?’ ‘Probably. It’ll be in my calendar somewhere. It was around the end of 2012.’ ‘If you could let me have that, it would be really helpful.’ Pause. ‘! don’t suppose Dr Ketterley happened to mention the name of this other person? The one who was getting him the information?’ ‘No.’ ‘Did he ever mention a man called Matthew Rose Sorensen?’ ‘No.’ “You're sure?’ ‘Yes.’ Second interview: Al Martineau, field recordist, 10 August 2018 ‘ ... if you ask someone at the front desk, they should be able to sort you out. OK. So my name’s Detective Sergeant Sarah Raphael. And I’m talking to Albert Martineau. Should I call you Albert or Al?’ ‘Al’s fine.’ ‘Perfect. What this is about is that in 2011 you saw Dr Ketterley as part of ... of treatment of some kind?’ ‘Treatment for depression. Yes.’ ‘So I'd like to talk to you about that and obviously we might be treading close to some personal matters and I need to stress again that you don’t need to talk about anything that makes you uncomfortable. And you don’t have to talk to me at all if you don’t want to.’ ‘It’s fine. I don’t mind talking about it.’ ‘Great. Thank you. The reason we’re interested in talking to you is because of an email you wrote on 21 November 2011 to the Psychiatric Services at St Dymphna’s Hospital in Ealing... ’ “Yes,” ‘... and in this email you expressed some concern about Dr Ketterley’s mode of therapy. Have I got that right?’ ‘Yes. But it’s important to say that about a week later I wrote a second email taking back what I'd said in the first email. I apologised for wasting their time.’ ‘Right. Would you mind telling me a little about what led up to you writing the first email?’ ‘Sure. I mean ... OK. It wasn’t anything really. It was just strange, I suppose. You don’t expect a therapist to do that. But I don’t want to get him into trouble. What’s this about exactly?’ ‘We’re looking into a disappearance. A disappearance which is possibly connected to several other disappearances.’ ‘Oh, OK. And this has something to do with Val?’ ‘Probably not. That’s what we’re trying to find out. He knew some of the people who disappeared.’ ‘I see. Well, the time you're talking about ... the reason I was seeing him was that I was going through a really bad time. I'd done something very stupid. I’d got involved with a woman who was a friend of my sister and who was married. I was twenty-four and she was forty-three. I thought it was this great love affair and it turned out ... it turned out it wasn’t. And in one fell swoop all my confidence flew out of the window. I’d hurt my family and I'd really misjudged the other person. She wasn’t who I thought she was. It was a huge shock. I started having panic attacks. I couldn’t concentrate on anything. I lost my job. And so on and so forth.’ Pause. ‘I’d been to a couple of private therapists and that hadn’t worked out and then my GP referred me to the Psychiatric Services at St Dymphna’s and they sent me to Val. And I quite liked him. At least I liked him to begin with. He was very cool, very professional and by that point I’d had enough of warm, friendly people. Warm, friendly people betrayed you. That was what | thought. So I found his detachment quite soothing, I suppose. Our sessions went pretty well and then one time I mentioned that I was interested in field recordings. It’s a big thing of mine. And Val said ... ’ ‘Sorry, what? Field what?’ ‘Recordings. It’s where you take sound recording equip- ment and you go out and make recordings of whatever’s going on in the world.’ ‘Like bird recordings?’ ‘Could be birds. That’s usually the part people are most familiar with. But it needn’t be birds. It could be anything. A suspension bridge in a high wind. A garden on a hot day with
